content.text: MCP resource templates are URI patterns, following RFC 6570, that a server declares to tell clients it can serve resources at an infinite set of addresses — not just a fixed list. Instead of enumerating every possible GitHub issue or database row in resources/list, the server publishes a template like github://repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number} and resolves any URI that matches it on demand.

Static resources are enumerated upfront and suit a small, known set of items (config files, a README, a schema). Resource templates suit datasets where the address space is unbounded: every row in a table, every commit in a repository, every user profile in a service.
fixed URIs returned by resources/list; server knows every item ahead of time.
URI patterns with {variable} slots; client resolves a concrete URI and calls resources/read.
the URI Template specification defines the expansion syntax — the same standard used by OpenAPI and HAL.

attrs.alt: MCP Resource Templates: URI patterns vs static resources, resolution flow

content.text: Declaring Templates in the Python SDK
A server registers resource templates using the @app.list_resource_templates() decorator. Each template is a ResourceTemplate object with a uriTemplate, name, mimeType, and optional description:

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.stdio import stdio_server
from mcp import types

app = Server("github-resources")

@app.list_resources()
async def list_resources() -> list[types.Resource]:
    # Static resources — always available
    return [
        types.Resource(
            uri="github://rate-limit",
            name="Rate Limit Status",
            mimeType="application/json",
            description="Current GitHub API rate limit"
        )
    ]

@app.list_resource_templates()
async def list_resource_templates() -> list[types.ResourceTemplate]:
    return [
        types.ResourceTemplate(
            uriTemplate="github://repos/{owner}/{repo}",
            name="GitHub Repository",
            mimeType="application/json",
            description="Repository metadata for {owner}/{repo}"
        ),
        types.ResourceTemplate(
            uriTemplate="github://repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}",
            name="GitHub Issue",
            mimeType="application/json",
            description="Issue #{number} in {owner}/{repo}"
        ),
        types.ResourceTemplate(
            uriTemplate="github://repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{+path}",
            name="Repository File",
            mimeType="text/plain",
            description="File at {path} in {owner}/{repo} (use + for paths with slashes)"
        ),
    ]

async def main():
    async with stdio_server() as (read, write):
        await app.run(read, write, app.create_initialization_options())

import asyncio
asyncio.run(main())

When a client requests a resource whose URI matches a template, it calls resources/read with the fully expanded URI. The server's @app.read_resource() handler receives the URI and must parse the variables back out:

import re
import httpx

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]

REPO_PATTERN = re.compile(r"^github://repos/([^/]+)/([^/]+)$")
ISSUE_PATTERN = re.compile(r"^github://repos/([^/]+)/([^/]+)/issues/(\d+)$")
CONTENTS_PATTERN = re.compile(r"^github://repos/([^/]+)/([^/]+)/contents/(.+)$")

@app.read_resource()
async def read_resource(uri: str) -> types.ReadResourceResult:
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}"}

    if uri == "github://rate-limit":
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.get("https://api.github.com/rate_limit", headers=headers)
            r.raise_for_status()
        return types.ReadResourceResult(
            contents=[types.TextResourceContents(uri=uri, text=r.text, mimeType="application/json")]
        )

    m = REPO_PATTERN.match(uri)
    if m:
        owner, repo = m.groups()
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.get(f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}", headers=headers)
            r.raise_for_status()
        return types.ReadResourceResult(
            contents=[types.TextResourceContents(uri=uri, text=r.text, mimeType="application/json")]
        )

    m = ISSUE_PATTERN.match(uri)
    if m:
        owner, repo, number = m.groups()
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.get(f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}", headers=headers)
            r.raise_for_status()
        return types.ReadResourceResult(
            contents=[types.TextResourceContents(uri=uri, text=r.text, mimeType="application/json")]
        )

    m = CONTENTS_PATTERN.match(uri)
    if m:
        owner, repo, path = m.groups()
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.get(f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}", headers=headers)
            r.raise_for_status()
        data = r.json()
        import base64
        content_text = base64.b64decode(data["content"]).decode()
        return types.ReadResourceResult(
            contents=[types.TextResourceContents(uri=uri, text=content_text, mimeType="text/plain")]
        )

    raise ValueError(f"Unknown resource URI: {uri}")
Resource template URIs follow RFC 6570. The most important operators for MCP are:
Reserved expansion; slashes and colons not encoded

Use {+path} instead of {path} whenever the variable can contain forward slashes (file paths, URL paths). {path} would percent-encode the slashes as %2F, making the URI unreadable and harder to match server-side.

# WRONG — {path} encodes slashes
"file://workspace/{path}"
# Resolves to: file://workspace/src%2Flib%2Futils.py

# CORRECT — {+path} preserves slashes
"file://workspace/{+path}"
# Resolves to: file://workspace/src/lib/utils.py
Resource templates are natural for exposing individual rows from a database without listing the entire table:

import asyncpg
import json
import re
import os

app = Server("postgres-resources")
DB_URL = os.environ["DATABASE_URL"]

@app.list_resource_templates()
async def list_resource_templates() -> list[types.ResourceTemplate]:
    return [
        types.ResourceTemplate(
            uriTemplate="postgres://tables/{schema}/{table}",
            name="Table Schema",
            mimeType="application/json",
            description="Column definitions for {schema}.{table}"
        ),
        types.ResourceTemplate(
            uriTemplate="postgres://rows/{schema}/{table}/{id}",
            name="Table Row",
            mimeType="application/json",
            description="Row with primary key {id} from {schema}.{table}"
        ),
    ]

TABLE_PATTERN = re.compile(r"^postgres://tables/([\w]+)/([\w]+)$")
ROW_PATTERN = re.compile(r"^postgres://rows/([\w]+)/([\w]+)/([\w\-]+)$")

@app.read_resource()
async def read_resource(uri: str) -> types.ReadResourceResult:
    conn = await asyncpg.connect(DB_URL)
    try:
        m = TABLE_PATTERN.match(uri)
        if m:
            schema, table = m.groups()
            # Validate identifiers before using in query
            if not re.match(r'^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$', schema) or not re.match(r'^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$', table):
                raise ValueError("Invalid schema or table name")
            rows = await conn.fetch(
                "SELECT column_name, data_type FROM information_schema.columns "
                "WHERE table_schema=$1 AND table_name=$2 ORDER BY ordinal_position",
                schema, table
            )
            return types.ReadResourceResult(contents=[types.TextResourceContents(
                uri=uri,
                text=json.dumps([dict(r) for r in rows], indent=2),
                mimeType="application/json"
            )])

        m = ROW_PATTERN.match(uri)
        if m:
            schema, table, row_id = m.groups()
            if not re.match(r'^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$', schema) or not re.match(r'^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$', table):
                raise ValueError("Invalid schema or table name")
            # Use parameterised query for the id value
            row = await conn.fetchrow(
                f'SELECT * FROM "{schema}"."{table}" WHERE id=$1', row_id
            )
            if row is None:
                raise ValueError(f"Row {row_id} not found in {schema}.{table}")
            return types.ReadResourceResult(contents=[types.TextResourceContents(
                uri=uri,
                text=json.dumps(dict(row), indent=2, default=str),
                mimeType="application/json"
            )])

        raise ValueError(f"Unknown resource URI: {uri}")
    finally:
        await conn.close()

Always validate schema and table name identifiers against a strict allowlist pattern before interpolating them into SQL. Even with parameterised queries for values, identifiers cannot be parameterised and must be validated manually.

Clients can subscribe to resource URIs (including template-resolved ones) to receive notifications when the underlying data changes. The server sends a notifications/resources/updated notification with the URI:

import asyncio

# Map of URI → set of subscriber session IDs (simplified)
_subscribers: dict[str, set] = {}

@app.subscribe_resource()
async def on_subscribe(uri: str):
    ctx = app.request_context
    session_id = id(ctx.session)
    _subscribers.setdefault(uri, set()).add(session_id)
    return types.EmptyResult()

@app.unsubscribe_resource()
async def on_unsubscribe(uri: str):
    ctx = app.request_context
    session_id = id(ctx.session)
    _subscribers.get(uri, set()).discard(session_id)
    return types.EmptyResult()

async def notify_changed(uri: str, session):
    """Call this when underlying data for a URI changes."""
    await session.send_resource_updated(uri)

# Example: poll a GitHub issue for changes every 60 seconds
async def poll_issue(owner: str, repo: str, number: str, session):
    uri = f"github://repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}"
    last_updated = None
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(60)
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.get(
                f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{number}",
                headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['GITHUB_TOKEN']}"}
            )
        data = r.json()
        updated_at = data.get("updated_at")
        if updated_at != last_updated:
            last_updated = updated_at
            if uri in _subscribers:
                await notify_changed(uri, session)

A common pattern is exposing data through both resources (for direct reading by the model) and tools (for filtered, parameterised queries). Resources give the model a way to dereference a known URI; tools give it a way to discover and search:

import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch

async def test_read_repo_resource():
    fake_response = {"id": 123, "name": "hello-world", "full_name": "octocat/hello-world"}

    with patch("httpx.AsyncClient") as mock_client_cls:
        mock_client = mock_client_cls.return_value.__aenter__.return_value
        mock_client.get = AsyncMock(return_value=AsyncMock(
            text=json.dumps(fake_response),
            raise_for_status=lambda: None
        ))
        with patch.dict(os.environ, {"GITHUB_TOKEN": "test-token"}):
            result = await read_resource("github://repos/octocat/hello-world")

    assert result.contents[0].mimeType == "application/json"
    data = json.loads(result.contents[0].text)
    assert data["name"] == "hello-world"

async def test_unknown_uri_raises():
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Unknown resource URI"):
        await read_resource("github://unknown/path")

async def test_list_resource_templates():
    templates = await list_resource_templates()
    uris = [t.uriTemplate for t in templates]
    assert any("{owner}" in u and "{repo}" in u for u in uris)

MCP resource templates use RFC 6570 URI patterns to expose unbounded address spaces — every row in a database, every file in a repository, every entity in an API — without enumerating them upfront. Servers declare templates via list_resource_templates(), parse the resolved URIs with regex in read_resource(), and validate all identifier components before using them in queries. Use {+path} for path segments that contain slashes. Templates compose naturally with tools: tools discover and search, resources dereference known addresses.
